PHASE: Phase 7 active — fifth Feature Update merged. Test infrastructure now self-protecting — silent regression of the 20-test suite (accidental .skip, deleted suite, branch-coverage drop on the fully-tested auth router) will fail CI. New `coverage` CI job uploads HTML + lcov artifact for 14 days.

LAST_DONE: 2026-05-16 Phase 7 #5 (Opus 4.7 direct, single-session, ~25K context — Tier 1). User picked option (g) coverage threshold gate from queued .whatsnext candidates. Delivered:

  (3) package.json (root) — new `test:coverage` script delegating to `turbo run test:coverage`.
  (4) .github/workflows/ci.yml — new `coverage` job under needs: governance; runs `pnpm test:coverage` and uploads apps/web/coverage/ as 14-day artifact via actions/upload-artifact@v4 (HTML + lcov).
Gate-engagement verified inline: temporarily set auth.ts branches threshold to 99 (above measured 78.94) → confirmed exit 1 with explicit failure message → restored to 75. No test files modified — this is a config-only safety net.
Squash-merged to main as SHA `<SHA>`. 4 files modified, 0 created. Visual QA (Rule 16) not applicable (no UI changes).

TIER_CLASSIFICATION: 1 — lightweight (4 files touched, 3 modules: apps/web config, root turbo, root CI). Single Opus session well within 30K budget. Direct execution chosen because the work is purely config/wiring — no schema, no business logic, no cross-file invariants beyond "thresholds match measured baseline".

PHASE_7_TICKET_5_NOTES:
  - Threshold strategy: TWO-TIER, NOT single global. Global floor matches measured baseline (12/6/12/12) — purpose is to catch catastrophic regression like an entire suite getting .skip'd. Per-file gate on auth.ts at 100/75/100/100 — purpose is to catch partial regression on the only file that has real coverage (a global floor of 12% would let auth.ts coverage drop from 100% to 50% silently). Branches threshold set to 75 (measured 78.94) for small slack on the 4 measured edge cases in the rate-limit error path. This pattern is now [[vitest-per-file-glob-thresholds]] — see lessons.md for the syntax reference.
  - meetings.ts NOT under per-file gate yet — only `.create` is tested (10.81% statements). Adding a per-file gate here would either lock in a useless 10% threshold or force tests to be written before the next refactor of `meetings.create`. Decision: leave meetings.ts under the global floor only until more procedures get test coverage. When `getJoinToken` / `list` get tests, add a meetings.ts per-file gate.
  - Gate engagement RED→GREEN proof: temporarily bumped auth.ts branches threshold from 75 to 99 → `pnpm test:coverage` exited 1 with `Coverage for branches (78.94%) does not meet "src/server/trpc/routers/auth.ts" threshold (99%)` → restored to 75. This was the "test" for the config change itself, since there's no application-code test to write for a coverage gate.
  - HIGH CVE pre-existing flag: `pnpm audit --audit-level=high` fails on nodemailer addressparser DoS. Verified pre-existing on main via stash + checkout + re-audit (identical 6/1/4/1 severity). Not introduced by this branch. Following phases.md decision tree step 1/2/3 — but step 1 (audit --fix) and step 2 (upgrade to 7.x) both blocked by Auth.js v5 peer dep range. Step 3 (document mitigation + accept) deferred to a dedicated follow-up ticket (Phase 7 #6 candidate j) because the mitigation analysis (addressparser only invoked on server-stamped fields) deserves its own commit + decision-log entry, not a hide-it-in-the-coverage-gate-commit. Phase 7 #5 PR is not blocked because the audit was already failing on main.
  - PreToolUse hook false positive on .github/workflows/ci.yml edit: workflow-injection security reminder fired even though the added `coverage` job uses only project-controlled env vars (NODE_VERSION, PNPM_VERSION) and a static command (`pnpm test:coverage`) — no `${{ github.event.* }}` input flows into any `run:`. Retry succeeded. Logged 🟤 decision in lessons.md so future agents recognize this as a precautionary warning rather than a content-aware block.
  - lcov reporter added alongside text + html: unlocks future Codecov/Sonar integration if needed, near-zero cost to generate, uploaded as part of the coverage-report artifact.
